summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Paul Graham's essay comparing American culture to software development, emphasizing that good programming is a messy, fast-moving process rather than a methodical one. The post argues that working slowly and meticulously on software is actually counterproductive—a form of premature optimization. Instead, programmers should build prototypes quickly to discover new ideas and avoid polishing mistaken initial concepts. Atwood distills this into his characteristic blunt advice: good programmers take action rather than overthinking.
key_points: Good software development is messy and iterative, not systematic and planned like building pyramids
Working slowly and meticulously is premature optimization—you end up with a polished version of a wrong idea
Fast prototyping leads to better insights than careful upfront planning
Good craftsmanship in software paradoxically means working fast, not slow
money_quotes: Code is not something they assemble painstakingly after careful planning, like the pyramids. It's something they plunge into, working fast and constantly changing their minds, like a charcoal sketch.
In software, paradoxical as it sounds, good craftsmanship means working fast.
If you work slowly and meticulously, you merely end up with a very fine implementation of your initial, mistaken idea.
Working slowly and meticulously is premature optimization.
Better to get a prototype done fast, and see what new ideas it gives you.
key_insight: In software, speed enables learning—building fast prototypes reveals better ideas than carefully implementing your first flawed concept.
